commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.

learningObjectives.text: Explain arrays and objects using a new example and justify the result.
Explain sets and maps using a new example and justify the result.
Choose and justify selection criteria and trade-offs using a new example and justify the result.

mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Arrays and objects** → **Sets and maps** → **Selection criteria and trade-offs**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

choices.feedback: Arrays and objects is best understood as follows: Arrays model ordered sequences; objects model records with named properties. Choose the shape that matches the data and operations.
choices.label: Arrays model ordered sequences; objects model records with named properties. Choose the shape that matches the data and operations.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Arrays and objects.
choices.label: Exports define a module's public API; imports declare required values. Narrow APIs and acyclic dependencies improve maintainability.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Arrays and objects.
choices.label: Array filter returns a new array containing only elements whose predicate is truthy.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Arrays and objects.
choices.label: AJAX is the practice of exchanging data without replacing the whole page. An API defines endpoints, inputs, outputs, status codes, and error behavior.

questions.explanation: Arrays and objects is best understood as follows: Arrays model ordered sequences; objects model records with named properties. Choose the shape that matches the data and operations.
questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “Arrays and objects” in the context of Choosing the Right Data Structure?

choices.feedback: Sets and maps is best understood as follows: A Set models unique values and membership. A Map models an explicit key-value collection with flexible keys and predictable iteration.
choices.label: A Set models unique values and membership. A Map models an explicit key-value collection with flexible keys and predictable iteration.

choices.feedback: Selection criteria and trade-offs is best understood as follows: Choose a data structure by the dominant operations: ordered iteration, lookup, uniqueness, insertion, deletion, and serialization. No structure is universally best.
choices.label: Choose a data structure by the dominant operations: ordered iteration, lookup, uniqueness, insertion, deletion, and serialization. No structure is universally best.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.

choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
choices.label: Apply Arrays and objects deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.
questions.prompt: A learner must use Choosing the Right Data Structure in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.feedback: The correct choice connects the example to the underlying model rather than treating the syntax as isolated trivia.
choices.label: An array stores order, a Map provides keyed lookup, and a Set enforces uniqueness.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: The snippet removes the need to understand the data flowing through the program.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: The snippet guarantees that all values are immutable and all operations are asynchronous.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.

questions.explanation: The correct choice connects the example to the underlying model rather than treating the syntax as isolated trivia.
questions.hint: Trace data, control flow, ownership, or rendering step by step.
questions.prompt: What is the most accurate explanation of the following Choosing the Right Data Structure example?

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: The statement is correct only when variable names are short.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: The statement is always correct, so no further reasoning about Choosing the Right Data Structure is needed.

choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.
choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.

questions.explanation: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.
questions.hint: Look for the option that corrects both the belief and the learner's next action.
questions.prompt: Which response best addresses this common mistake: “Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.”?

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.

choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Arrays and objects, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.
questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Choosing the Right Data Structure?

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: Avoid Choosing the Right Data Structure completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: Use Choosing the Right Data Structure in every file because more abstraction is always better.

choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
choices.label: Use Choosing the Right Data Structure when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Choosing the Right Data Structure.
choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Choosing the Right Data Structure?

summary: Choosing the Right Data Structure connects Arrays and objects, Sets and maps, Selection criteria and trade-offs.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.
title: Choosing the Right Data Structure

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

whyThisMatters: Learners frequently use choosing the right data structure without a clear model of why it behaves as it does; this lesson makes that model explicit. It belongs to the **Data Structures and Functional Techniques** module.
